raw_data:
b' Matt Levine's Money Stuff
I have previously expressed my fondness for AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc.\xe2\x80\x99s APEs scheme. AMC was a big meme stock and had a lot of investo

Apes vs. APEs

I have previously expressed my fondness for AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc.\xe2\x80\x99s APEs\xc2\xa0scheme. AMC was a big meme stock and had\xc2\xa0a lot of investors who wanted to buy its stock; it also needed a lot of money and was happy to sell stock to them. But its corporate charter only allowed it to issue about 524 million common shares, and AMC was running out of stock to sell. It asked its shareholders to authorize more shares but \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0well, they didn\xe2\x80\x99t exactly say no. What happened is that AMC withdrew the request for more shares because it couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get enough votes, and it couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get enough votes in part because its existing shareholders were worried about dilution, but also in (probably larger) part because AMC\xe2\x80\x99s overwhelmingly retail investor base simply doesn\xe2\x80\x99t vote. Retail shareholders tend not to vote much on anything; they are hard to reach, and voting your shares is confusing and pointless enough that ordinary retail investors rarely do it.

But AMC needed a majority of all of its shares to vote to authorize new shares; not voting is equivalent to voting no. So AMC had people who wanted to buy stock, it wanted to sell them stock, and it (probably) thought that its shareholders wanted it to issue more stock \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but it couldn\xe2\x80\x99t reach those shareholders to get them to vote to approve more stock.

So AMC came up with a clever trick, the APEs, AMC Preferred Equity Units. It created a new class of preferred stock; each preferred share has the economic rights of 100 shares of common stock, gets 100 votes and votes together with the common stock on everything. Then it gave its shareholders 1/100th of a preferred share \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0an APE \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0for each common share, creating sort of a stock split. Then it started selling more APEs to raise cash, and sold a big block of APEs to a hedge fund called Antara Capital. And now it is asking its shareholders \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0APE holders and common shareholders, all voting together \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0to approve a charter amendment to authorize new shares, do a reverse share split and convert the APEs into common shares. This vote should succeed, because:

  1. There are now many more APEs than common shares, and the APE holders have an incentive to vote yes. (APEs trade at a big discount to common shares, and if the APEs are converted that discount will naturally close.)
  2. APEs are more likely to be held by professional arbitrageurs, not retail investors, so they are more likely to vote.
  3. A big block of APEs is held by Antara, which has signed an agreement to vote its shares in favor of the amendment.
  4. The preferred shares underlying the APEs are held by a depositary, Computershare Trust Co., which has agreed to vote all of the preferred shares in proportion to how any voting APEs vote. So if 20% of the APEs vote yes, 5% vote no, and 75% don\xe2\x80\x99t vote at all, Computershare will vote 80% of the preferred stock for yes, which will be enough to pass the amendments even if all of the common stock doesn\xe2\x80\x99t vote (or votes no).

AMC\xe2\x80\x99s problem was that its retail investors don\xe2\x80\x99t vote, and it came up with a solution. I like it!

Not everyone does. Before the APEs, it probably made sense for AMC\xe2\x80\x99s common shareholders to vote to authorize more shares: AMC needed money, and if it could sell shares at high prices to new investors, that was good for existing shareholders. (If you are selling stock at prices higher than fundamental value, that\xe2\x80\x99s accretive , not dilutive. [1] ) A rational AMC shareholder, pre-APEs, probably should have wanted to authorize more shares, even if the shareholders\xc2\xa0did not bother to vote to make that happen.

But now that the APEs are outstanding, the common shareholders might rationally not want to convert them into common stock. The APEs closed on Friday at $2.40 per APE; the common stock closed at $5.24. When the APEs convert into common, those prices will converge. It\xe2\x80\x99s not entirely clear if the final price should be closer to $2.40 or $5.24, but it\xe2\x80\x99s plausible that it would be somewhere in the middle, meaning that voting for the conversion would reduce the value of the common stock. If you are a common shareholder, converting all the APEs into common stock definitely is dilutive, and you might not like it. [2] You might vote against authorizing new shares. That doesn\xe2\x80\x99t matter very much: The APEs have a majority of the voting power and are very incentivized to vote for their own conversion. But you might be mad.

You might sue. Here is a lawsuit against AMC that was filed in Delaware Chancery Court yesterday:

This Action challenges a course of complex and disloyal corporate engineering by the Defendants\xe2\x80\x94described by AMC\xe2\x80\x99s Chief Executive Officer and Chairman, Defendant Adam M. Aron (\xe2\x80\x9cAron\xe2\x80\x9d) as an exercise in \xe2\x80\x9c3-D chess\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 devised to achieve a simple aim: eviscerating the voting power of AMC\xe2\x80\x99s Class A stockholders in order to force through approval of a proposed dilutive share count increase that those stockholders repeatedly had rebuffed and were not willing to support at the corporate ballot box. ...

Seizing on increases in AMC\xe2\x80\x99s stock price driven by retail investors, AMC sold nearly all of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s remaining authorized shares of Class A common stock to raise capital. Thereafter, AMC proposed on multiple occasions to amend the Company\xe2\x80\x99s certificate of incorporation to authorize the issuance of additional shares of Class A common stock\xe2\x80\x94but those proposals were rebuffed repeatedly by the Company\xe2\x80\x99s existing Class A common stockholders, who twice forced the withdrawal of such proposals.

In response, AMC embarked on a course of complex corporate engineering for the plain and obvious purpose of circumventing the will of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s existing stockholders and securing a dilutive increase in the number of authorized Class A shares over their unambiguous opposition. \xe2\x80\xa6

With outstanding APEs outnumbering the Class A common shares by nearly 2 to 1 and the Depositary Voting Requirement supercharging the APEs\xe2\x80\x99 voting power as a class vis-\xc3\xa0-vis AMC\xe2\x80\x99s Class A stockholders, AMC is poised to finally force through the Class A share count increase that was opposed repeatedly by Class A stockholders before AMC and the Board took steps to reconstitute their electorate.

AMC and the Board presumably believe their game of \xe2\x80\x9c3-D chess\xe2\x80\x9d against the Class A stockholders is approaching checkmate. But, as detailed herein, the vote cannot be allowed to proceed as planned. In their zeal to circumvent the will of the Class A stockholders, Defendants have breached their fiduciary duties and violated the [Delaware General Corporation\xc2\xa0Law].

There are two main arguments. One is sort of a technical one: Delaware law says that the common shareholders have to get to vote on any charter amendment that would \xe2\x80\x9calter or change the powers, preferences, or special rights of the shares of such class so as to affect them adversely,\xe2\x80\x9d and the shareholders here argue that the issuance of the APEs reduced their rights and so should have been put to a shareholder vote. [3] The APEs are what is called \xe2\x80\x9cblank-check preferred stock,\xe2\x80\x9d meaning that AMC\xe2\x80\x99s corporate charter allows the board of directors to create and issue new preferred stock with whatever terms they like, but the argument here is that creating the APEs reduced the rights of the common and so separately violated the law:

At the time the Board created the Preferred Shares, AMC had only one class of authorized stock with voting rights, its Class A common stock. The right to vote on matters requiring stockholder approval was not shared with any other class. Thus, the right to vote was a special characteristic of the common stock in the capital structure of AMC.

Specifically, the sole or primary purpose of the Preferred Stock\xe2\x80\x99s creation and issuance was to eviscerate the voting power of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Class A\xc2\xa0stockholders and effectively fashion a new electorate that, unlike the pre-existing stockholder base, would not rebuff the Board\xe2\x80\x99s proposals to issue additional common stock. That is, before the creation of the Preferred Stock, AMC\xe2\x80\x99s Class A stockholders had the power and right to reject a specific proposal desired by the Board. This power and right conflicted with the desires of the Board, so the Board created and issued the Preferred Stock for the purpose of removing that power and right from the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Class A stockholders.

I am not really convinced \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0if this is right, then that means that boards can never issue\xc2\xa0voting preferred stock without shareholder approval, which might make sense but would come as\xc2\xa0a surprise \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but you can see the appeal of it. It is strange to think that\xc2\xa0boards could issue new shares with unlimited voting power without shareholder approval.

The other argument is that the board violated its fiduciary duty to shareholders: Even if the technical mechanics of the APEs all work, AMC created the APEs to get around its shareholders\xe2\x80\x99 preferences, which seems icky and disloyal. From the complaint:

The Director Defendants approved the creation and sale of the Preferred Stock, imposed the Depositary Voting Requirement, and arranged for the impending vote on the Proposals all for the purpose of circumventing the will of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s existing stockholders, who previously had rejected proposals by the Board to increase the total number of authorized shares of Class A common stock\xe2\x80\x94essentially creating a new electorate\xc2\xa0and new voting rules, simply because they did not have the votes to get what they wanted. All of these steps were taken for the very purpose of reversing the likely outcome of Defendants\xe2\x80\x99 desired vote to increase the authorized number of Class A shares. This course of conduct constituted a breach of fiduciary duty.

Here again I think I sympathize with the board. It\xe2\x80\x99s their job to run the company. In the exercise of their\xc2\xa0fiduciary duty, thinking about what is best for the company and its shareholders, they figured the company would be better off issuing more stock. Their reasoning for that \xe2\x80\x94 that the company\xc2\xa0needed money and\xc2\xa0people wanted to buy the stock at $60 ! \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0was, I think,\xc2\xa0impeccable. [4] But they couldn\xe2\x80\x99t do it, because they couldn\xe2\x80\x99t get shareholder approval, in part because shareholders disagreed with them but in large part because the shareholders simply didn\xe2\x80\x99t vote. The company had a problem, its shareholders could not get their act together to solve it, so the board solved it for them. That\xe2\x80\x99s what the board is supposed to do, and Delaware courts do tend to be pretty sympathetic to boards of directors who make what they think is the right decision for their company even if the shareholders don\xe2\x80\x99t like it.

But they are less sympathetic to boards who try to take voting rights away from shareholders, and there is something about the APEs that feels like cheating. So I don\xe2\x80\x99t know what will happen. The special meeting to authorize new shares and convert the APEs into common is scheduled for March 14 ; the complaining shareholders\xc2\xa0have asked to postpone it for 60 days while they argue to block it in court.

One other point here. Shareholder class action lawsuits like this are now usually filed by institutional investors, often municipal retirement funds. [5] The lead plaintiff here \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the shareholder who brought the lawsuit \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0is the\xc2\xa0Allegheny County Employees\xe2\x80\x99 Retirement System, a pretty normal shareholder class-action plaintiff, yet one who is somehow unrepresentative of AMC\xe2\x80\x99s meme-stock shareholders. Really you want a pseudonymous individual Reddit poster to lead the lawsuit.

Regional Health

There seems to have been a wave of companies recently that are using blank-check preferred stock to get around a lack of enthusiasm for shareholder voting. AMC is the highest-profile example, but we talked last week about Soligenix Inc. , which had a different approach: It gave all shareholders a dividend of a special preferred stock that gave them an extra 1,000 votes per common share, but the preferred automatically disappears (1) right before the shareholder vote, if it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t vote, or (2) right after the shareholder vote, if it does vote. So at the time of the vote, the only shares that are outstanding are the ones that vote, [6] so you never face the problem where a majority of the shareholders who actually vote vote for the thing you want, but most shareholders don\xe2\x80\x99t vote at all so it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t\xc2\xa0pass.

This is, I think, a bit weirder than the AMC approach, but also less aggressive; AMC\xe2\x80\x99s approach involved placing big chunks of new stock with sympathetic holders, while this one is just sort of an accounting trick. Anyway it\xe2\x80\x99s now a trend; Regional Health Properties did one last Friday :

Regional Health Properties, Inc. \xe2\x80\xa6 announced today that its Board of Directors declared a dividend of one one-thousandth (1/1,000th) of a share of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s newly-designated Series E Redeemable Preferred Shares, no par value per share (the \xe2\x80\x9cSeries E Preferred Stock\xe2\x80\x9d), for each outstanding share of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s common stock (\xe2\x80\x9cCommon Stock\xe2\x80\x9d), payable on February 28, 2023 to shareholders of record as of 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time on February 27, 2023. The outstanding shares of Series E Preferred Stock will vote together with the outstanding shares of Common Stock, as a single class, exclusively with respect to (a) any proposal submitted to holders of Common Stock to amend the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Amended and Restated Articles of Incorporation to (i) make certain changes to the terms of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s 10.875% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Shares and (ii) temporarily increase the authorized number of shares of the Company (including the authorized shares of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s preferred stock) (the \xe2\x80\x9cCharter Amendment Proposal\xe2\x80\x9d) .... Subject to certain limitations, each outstanding share of Series E Preferred Stock will have 1,000,000 votes per share (or 1,000 votes per one one-thousandth of a share of Series E Preferred Stock).

All shares of Series E Preferred Stock that are not present in person or by proxy at any meeting of shareholders held to vote on the above-described proposals as of immediately prior to the opening of the polls on the Charter Amendment Proposal at such meeting will automatically be redeemed by the Company. Any outstanding shares of Series E Preferred Stock that have not been so redeemed will be redeemed if such redemption is ordered by the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Board of Directors or automatically upon the approval by the Company\xe2\x80\x99s shareholders of the Charter Amendment Proposal.

On the one hand it seems like a good solution. On the other hand, the law says that this sort of thing \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0here, approving the issuance of new shares \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0requires the approval of a majority of the outstanding\xc2\xa0shares, not just a majority of the shares that vote. If you can magically get around that requirement with this sort of accounting gimmick, then it\xe2\x80\x99s not much of a requirement.

Ensign Peak

The rule in the US is roughly\xc2\xa0that if you are an institution \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0a pension fund, an endowment, an investment firm, a bank, etc. \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and you own more than $100 million worth of stocks, you have to disclose your stock holdings every quarter. [7] You file Form 13F with the US Securities and Exchange Commission, and it is publicly available and everyone can see what stocks you own.

If you don\xe2\x80\x99t like this, there are reasonably standard solutions. The rule is actually that you have to file Form 13F if you \xe2\x80\x9cexercise investment discretion\xe2\x80\x9d over your stocks \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0meaning basically that you decide what stocks to buy and sell [8] \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0so the trick is not to do that. If you have $1 billion to invest in the stock market, you go to a big institutional investment manager and say \xe2\x80\x9chi, here\xe2\x80\x99s $1 billion, please buy some stocks for me.\xe2\x80\x9d You agree on some investment criteria \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the mandate for your investment \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and you pay the manager some fees, and the manager buys the stocks for you. This saves you all sorts of aggravation \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0picking the stocks, doing the trading, setting up custody arrangements, etc. \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but it also probably saves you from filing a Form 13F, if that\xe2\x80\x99s your goal. Instead, the investment management firm files a Form 13F showing all of the stocks that it owns \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that it \xe2\x80\x9cexercises investment discretion\xe2\x80\x9d over \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0for its various clients, including you. You are just an anonymous client; your data is aggregated with the rest of the clients.

Presumably if you\xe2\x80\x99re a big enough account, and interested enough in the markets, you might end up doing some kibbitzing about what your investment manager is buying. The manager\xe2\x80\x99s mandate for your account might be narrow and customized for\xc2\xa0your interests; if you are, for instance, a fund that invests money for a religious group, you might tell the manager\xc2\xa0to avoid certain sorts of sin stocks. You might chat periodically with the manager about what they\xe2\x80\x99re up to, and you might give them some input, without quite \xe2\x80\x9cexercising investment discretion\xe2\x80\x9d over the account. The manager\xc2\xa0makes the investing decisions, but with your preferences in mind.

You could push this further. Hire an \xe2\x80\x9cinvestment manager\xe2\x80\x9d to buy and sell stocks for you, but just tell them exactly what to buy and sell. They\xe2\x80\x99re not really in the business of making the investment decisions; they\xe2\x80\x99re just in the business of sitting between you and the public Form 13F requirement. This way you get exactly the stocks you want, and presumably a fake investment manager is cheaper than a real one.

This is an imperfect solution, since your fake investment manager presumably won\xe2\x80\x99t have other clients, so they\xc2\xa0will file a Form 13F that exactly describes your own stock holdings. Your name won\xe2\x80\x99t be on it, which is good, but if your holdings are big enough then people might be curious. \xe2\x80\x9cWho is this Anonymous Investments LLC that owns $30 billion of stock and has one client,\xe2\x80\x9d they might ask. But if you\xe2\x80\x99re doing this you can hire a bunch of different \xe2\x80\x9cinvestment managers,\xe2\x80\x9d have each of them own some of your stocks, and split up the disclosure\xc2\xa0into a bunch of anonymous Form 13F filings none of which are big or interesting enough to attract attention.

This is illegal, though you can see how you might feel justified in doing it. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy should random strangers get to know what stocks I own,\xe2\x80\x9d you might think. That seems to have been what the Mormon Church thought :

The Securities and Exchange Commission today announced charges against Ensign Peak Advisers Inc., a non-profit entity operated by The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints to manage the Church\xe2\x80\x99s investments, for failing to file forms that would have disclosed the Church\xe2\x80\x99s equity investments, and for instead filing forms for shell companies that obscured the Church\xe2\x80\x99s portfolio and misstated Ensign Peak\xe2\x80\x99s control over the Church\xe2\x80\x99s investment decisions. The SEC also announced charges against the Church for causing these violations. To settle the charges, Ensign Peak agreed to pay a $4 million penalty and the Church agreed to pay a $1 million penalty.

The SEC\xe2\x80\x99s order finds that, from 1997 through 2019, Ensign Peak failed to file Forms 13F, the forms on which investment managers are required to disclose the value of certain securities they manage. According to the order, the Church was concerned that disclosure of its portfolio, which by 2018 grew to approximately $32 billion, would lead to negative consequences. To obscure the amount of the Church\xe2\x80\x99s portfolio, and with the Church\xe2\x80\x99s knowledge and approval, Ensign Peak created thirteen shell LLCs, ostensibly with locations throughout the U.S., and filed Forms 13F in the names of these LLCs rather than in Ensign Peak\xe2\x80\x99s name. The order finds that Ensign Peak maintained investment discretion over all relevant securities, that it controlled the shell companies, and that it directed nominee \xe2\x80\x9cbusiness managers,\xe2\x80\x9d most of whom were employed by the Church, to sign the Commission filings. The shell LLCs\xe2\x80\x99 Forms 13F misstated, among other things, that the LLCs had sole investment and voting discretion over the securities. In reality, the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s order finds, Ensign Peak retained control over all investment and voting decisions.

\xe2\x80\x9cThe Church was concerned that disclosure of the assets in the name of Ensign Peak, a known Church affiliate, would lead to negative consequences in light of the size of the Church\xe2\x80\x99s portfolio,\xe2\x80\x9d says the complaint , and you can see their point. The effect of the 13F rules here is mostly to make public that the church has a lot of money. But the rules are the rules, and setting up 13 shell LLCs to pretend to manage your money for you doesn\xe2\x80\x99t really work to get around them.

Principles

The term \xe2\x80\x9chedge fund\xe2\x80\x9d can refer to both (1) a small firm run by a single idiosyncratic genius with a gut instinct and a tiny pirate crew or (2) a gigantic institution that is in the business of identifying talented investors\xc2\xa0and systematically extracting alpha from them . At some point a lot of the former category transition into the latter category, and it can be awkward. If you started a hedge fund, and your firm grew into a financial services behemoth with thousands of employees and tens of billions of dollars under management, and you\xe2\x80\x99re now at retirement age, the tension is:

  • On the one hand, it is very important to transition your firm into an institution that can work without you: You owe it to your employees and to your clients to make sure that the firm will thrive after you leave; plus, you probably own a bunch of shares and would like them to continue to be valuable.
  • On the other hand it is your firm, created in your image, and these other people don\xe2\x80\x99t have your creative genius and are going to mess it up.

I enjoyed this New York Times article about how Ray Dalio, who has spent the last decade or so pretending to retire from Bridgewater Associates, [9] apparently did his most recent retirement\xc2\xa0by going door to door asking all of his employees for cash to get him to leave:

But Mr. Dalio wasted little time in telling colleagues that he was not interested in a simple passing of the baton, according to current and former employees. He approached dozens of top employees \xe2\x80\x94 at the time, Bridgewater had roughly 1,500 full-time staff members \xe2\x80\x94 and told them they would have to buy his shares with their own money, some of those employees said.

Mr. Dalio offered to arrange 10-year loans to those who lacked the funds to buy him out, according to two former employees. If they declined, he intimated that they should consider leaving the firm, they said. Still, even as he sold shares to his colleagues, decreasing his ownership, his founder shares kept him in control.

On the one hand, that is not really how big institutional financial firms operate; Goldman Sachs Group Inc. did not take up a collection to get Lloyd Blankfein to quit. On the other hand, while Bridgewater is a big institutional financial firm, it is also an extension of Ray Dalio, and you can see why it\xe2\x80\x99s hard for him to let go. \xe2\x80\x9cAt an internal meeting last year during the heat of the exit negotiations, Mr. Dalio described the hedge fund as his \xe2\x80\x98property rights,\xe2\x80\x99 according to one employee, and indicated that he expected to be compensated accordingly.\xe2\x80\x9d I mean, fair.

Anyway for now there are non-Dalio co-chief executive officers , Nir Bar Dea and Mark Bertolini, and I guess they are remaking it in their image?

In the meantime, Mr. Bar Dea has begun dismantling some parts of Mr. Dalio\xe2\x80\x99s legacy. Bridgewater dropped many of his \xe2\x80\x9cPrinciples,\xe2\x80\x9d and their associated ratings tools. Giving unvarnished feedback to one another \xe2\x80\x94 a stalwart of Mr. Dalio\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9cradical transparency\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 is now optional, current employees said.

Still, some of Mr. Bar Dea\xe2\x80\x99s changes have a hint of the hedge fund\xe2\x80\x99s old ways. He has recently taken to telling employees that he is coming up with his own list of \xe2\x80\x9cPrinciples.\xe2\x80\x9d

What? Surely that\xe2\x80\x99s the fastest way to get Dalio to un-retire again. Never mind the money; if you scrap Dalio\xe2\x80\x99s Principles and replace them with your own Principles and they are different principles \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0if you replace radical transparency with radical politeness? \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then he\xe2\x80\x99s going to be on you like a hyena on a wildebeest .

Top earning hedge fund manager

Really the best-performing hedge fund each year \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0as measured by percentage returns \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0always should be one that runs at 50x leverage :

Said Haidar\xe2\x80\x99s conviction that inflation was about to explode across the globe can be summarized by a single number: $63 billion. That\xe2\x80\x99s how much his Haidar Capital Management reported in assets to start 2022.

The catch? The hedge fund actually oversaw just $1.2 billion.

That copious leverage led to a tumultuous year \xe2\x80\x94 one month the fund was up 54%, another it was down 20% \xe2\x80\x94 but ultimately paid off, producing a 193% return for investors. Haidar bet big that interest rates would climb at a rapid clip, correctly positioning to profit from the surge in inflation that led to the most aggressive central bank tightening campaign in a generation. \xe2\x80\xa6

Haidar personally raked in $859 million in 2022, placing him sixth on Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s annual ranking of top-earning hedge fund managers. He\xe2\x80\x99s the newest and least-known name on a list otherwise dominated by industry heavyweights. Citadel\xe2\x80\x99s Ken Griffin, Point72\xe2\x80\x99s Steve Cohen and Millennium\xe2\x80\x99s Izzy Englander, worth about $55 billion combined, nabbed the first three spots.

Obviously the worst-performing hedge fund each year should be a different one that runs at 50x leverage. Anyway Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s list of top-earning hedge fund managers is mostly a list of\xc2\xa0huge multistrategy funds, suggesting that the hedge fund business is pretty institutionalized these days. But you still sometimes get a guy with his name on the door, a gut instinct and a ton of leverage breaking into the top ranks.

De-IPO

We talk sometimes about the basic phenomenon that (1) public-market prices move around a lot and (2) private-market prices don\xe2\x80\x99t. If you run a private real estate firm or a portfolio of private tech company stocks, and the price of publicly traded real estate companies or tech stocks falls by 50%, you might say \xe2\x80\x9chuh, shame about all those public companies having problems, but our portfolio is actually up 1% this year.\xe2\x80\x9d

One interpretation of this is that the fundamental value of your sector\xc2\xa0really did fall by 50%, and the public market price reflects the best possible consensus of that fact, and you are just able to ignore that because there is no publicly traded mark for your portfolio. Another, opposite interpretation is that the fundamental value hasn\xe2\x80\x99t changed, but the public market panicked for reasons of its own, and you \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0with your patient approach and absence of a trading price \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0can just consider fundamental value and ignore meaningless volatility.

In the latter case, private investors should want to buy public assets (and make them private) when the public market collapses, and sell private assets (to make them public) when the public market rallies. The Wall Street Journal reports :

A growing number of newly public companies are racing back to private ownership after discovering that an IPO isn\xe2\x80\x99t always all it\xe2\x80\x99s cracked up to be.

Of the hundreds of companies that went public in the boom years of 2020 and 2021, 10 have already agreed to sell themselves to private-equity firms, according to Dealogic. Of those that went public in 2018 or 2019, only eight have gone private in the ensuing years.

Driving the decision to opt for a buyout over remaining public in many cases is the dismal performance of the 2020-21 class of initial public offerings, the majority of which now trade below their debut prices. \xe2\x80\xa6

Cvent Holding Corp., which entered the public market in 2021 after years of ownership by buyout firm Vista Equity Partners, is exploring a sale that could value the event-software provider at upwards of $4 billion, The Wall Street Journal reported. Cvent had gone public by merging with a special-purpose acquisition company in a deal valued at $5.3 billion.

Yes, right,\xc2\xa0if you own a private company and there is a SPAC boom where people are desperate to give you cash to take it public at a high price, you should do that, and then buy it back cheap a year later. On the other hand the Journal also reports :

Fundraising by venture-capital firms hit a nine-year low in the fourth quarter, as the macroeconomic pressures that already weighed on technology startups began to affect the investors who underpin the industry.

Venture firms raised $20.6 billion in new funds in the fourth quarter. That was a 65% drop from the year-earlier quarter and the lowest fourth-quarter amount since 2013, according to data firm Preqin Ltd., which tracks venture-fund data. The amount was also less than half the level raised in the preceding three months, the first time fundraising volumes decreased from the third to fourth quarter since 2009, the data show.

[1] In fact AMC sold about 241.6 million shares for about $1.6 billion in 2021, for an average price of about $6.67, not much higher than today\xe2\x80\x99s share price (and today\xe2\x80\x99s price is after the 2-for-1-ish pseudo-stock-split caused by the APEs). But that average price is low because it was selling a lot in desperation in early 2021, and had only a few million shares left to sell by June 2021 when its stock was trading above $60 on meme energy. If shareholders had approved more shares *then*, as AMC\xe2\x80\x99s executives wanted, it could have hit that bid.

[2] A complicating factor here is that *not* voting to convert them, and just letting AMC issue more APEs, is also dilutive, since it is effectively selling common-stock-like securities at a huge discount to the price of the common stock.

[3] The law \xe2\x80\x94 DGCL section 242(b) \xe2\x80\x94 also says that shareholders get to vote as a class \xe2\x80\x9cif the amendment would increase or decrease the aggregate number of authorized shares of such class,\xe2\x80\x9d but a later sentence says that \xe2\x80\x9cThe number of authorized shares of any such class or classes of stock may be increased or decreased (but not below the number of shares thereof then outstanding) by the affirmative vote of the holders of a majority of the stock of the corporation entitled to vote irrespective of this subsection, if so provided in the original certificate of incorporation,\xe2\x80\x9d and AMC\xe2\x80\x99s charter does opt out of that requirement. So the shareholders here can\xe2\x80\x99t rely on *that* rule, but there is no exception for reducing the rights and powers of the class.

[4] Even the shareholder plaintiffs here kind of agree : \xe2\x80\x9cThe Board\xe2\x80\x99s decision to sell the authorized Class A common shares was enormously profitable\xe2\x80\x94raising over $1.8 billion in 2021. With this enormous influx of cash, the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Board paid down some of its debts, obtained an increase in the Company\xe2\x80\x99s credit rating, and embarked on an investment spree, greatly diversifying the Company\xe2\x80\x99s investments.\xe2\x80\x9d

[5] The reason is that the law says that the lead plaintiff \xe2\x80\x94 who does stuff like choose the lawyers and negotiate their fee \xe2\x80\x94 has to be the shareholder who is \xe2\x80\x9cmost capable of adequately representing the interests of class members\xe2\x80\x9d and who \xe2\x80\x9chas the largest financial interest\xe2\x80\x9d in the case, which tends to mean that it has to be a biggish professional institution, but the biggest asset managers mostly don\xe2\x80\x99t want to anger companies by leading lawsuits against them.

[6] Or, 99.9% of the shares are the ones that vote \xe2\x80\x94 common shares that don\xe2\x80\x99t vote still count as votes against the proposal, but they are swamped by the 1,000-vote preferred.

[7] More specifically, Rule 13f-1 provides that \xe2\x80\x9cEvery institutional investment manager which exercises investment discretion with respect to accounts holding section 13(f) securities \xe2\x80\xa6 having an aggregate fair market value on the last trading day of any month of any calendar year of at least $100,000,000 shall file a report on Form 13F \xe2\x80\xa6 within 45 days after the last day of such calendar year and within 45 days after the last day of each of the first three calendar quarters of the subsequent calendar year.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cSection 13(f) securities\xe2\x80\x9d means basically publicly traded stocks. \xe2\x80\x9c Institutional investment manager \xe2\x80\x9d means basically anyone other than an individual person trading their own personal account. So rich individuals trading their own accounts are not subject to the rule.

[8] The rules say : \xe2\x80\x9cA person exercises \xe2\x80\x98investment discretion\xe2\x80\x99 with respect to an account if, directly or indirectly, such person (A) is authorized to determine what securities or other property shall be purchased or sold by or for the account, (B) makes decisions as to what securities or other property shall be purchased or sold by or for the account even though some other person may have responsibility for such investment decisions, or (C) otherwise exercises such influence with respect to the purchase and sale of securities or other property by or for the account as the Commission, by rule, determines, in the public interest or for the protection of investors, should be subject to the operation of the provisions of this chapter and the rules and regulations thereunder.\xe2\x80\x9d

[9] Random fact: I am currently reading Jack Schwager\xe2\x80\x99s Hedge Fund Market Wizards, which features Dalio and notes that, when he was interviewed for the book in 2011, he had recently transitioned from running Bridgewater to serving as its \xe2\x80\x9cmentor.\xe2\x80\x9d This weekend\xe2\x80\x99s Times article describes a 2022 negotiation between Dalio and Bridgewater in which Dalio agreed that he would \xe2\x80\x9csurrender his titles, take on a new role as \xe2\x80\x98mentor to the C.I.O.s and investment committee,\xe2\x80\x99 and remain a member of the hedge fund\xe2\x80\x99s board.\xe2\x80\x9d Ray Dalio is in a perpetual state of having just agreed to resign as Bridgewater\xe2\x80\x99s CIO to become its mentor.
